overview.body: Remember all those distance constants? SAT_TO_CORPUS = 150, SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200, on and on? And those long if-elif chains for hazards? This week, all that hardcoded data moves into external files and gets loaded into dictionaries. One data file, one function to load it, and suddenly adding 50 more cities is just adding 50 lines to a text file. This is how real programs work.

mentorQuote.quote: If I asked you to add 20 new cities to the game using constants, how long would it take? Now imagine it's just adding 20 lines to a text file. That's why we use data files. Dictionaries let your code work with data that doesn't exist when you write it.

objectives: Write load_city_distances() to parse city_distances.txt into a nested dictionary
Write load_city_info() to parse city_info.txt into a dictionary with nested data
Display destinations and distances from dictionaries, not hardcoded values
Use .strip() and .split(',') to clean and parse file input
Wire all functions into a complete game loop with win detection and a coin flip gate
overview.keyConceptsText:
Chapter 8: String methods (.strip(), .split()) for parsing CSV data
Chapter 9: Dictionaries and nested dictionaries for structured data
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapters 8 & 9
programRequirements: Your program MUST load city data from external files, not hardcoded constants
Your program MUST use .strip() and .split(',') to parse file data
Your program MUST use dictionaries to store city distances and city info
Your program MUST use function_library.py for random functions and input validation
Your program MUST include a top-of-file docstring with name, abc123, section, and description
overview.saveYourCode: Keep lab-4-1.py — Module 4.2 adds string method input handling directly on top of your file-loading foundation.

dataFiles.format: Your shared function library from Module 3

overview.additionalFilesNote: Submit both lab-4-1.py and your accumulated function_library.py. CodeGrade tests your functions in isolation, so both files must be present.

rows.task: Parse file into dictionary with nested data

timeGuide.strategy: This is a BUILD week — focus on getting the data structure right before wiring it into the game. Test each loader function in isolation before moving to the next checkpoint.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. The [NEW] tags show what you're adding. [REPLACED] tags show what changes from your 3.3 code.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- DATA LOADING FUNCTIONS ----  [NEW]
load_city_distances()   reads city_distances.txt → nested dict
load_city_info()        reads city_info.txt → dict of dicts

# ---- NAVIGATION FUNCTIONS ----  [NEW]
display_destinations(city, distances)      shows menu from dict
get_destination_by_number(city, distances) validates user pick

# ---- MAIN PROGRAM ----
Load data files into dictionaries  [NEW]
Get player name
current_city = 'San Antonio'

GAME LOOP (each pass is one leg):
│
│── Display city info from city_info dict  [REPLACED] was hardcoded
│── Show destinations from distances dict  [REPLACED] was if/elif menu
│── Destination Gate (coin flip)
│
│   TRAVEL LOOP (each pass is one turn):
│   │── Fixed 50-mile travel, check for hazard
│   │── Repeat until you arrive
│
│── Update trip totals
│── Check outcome: beach / dead end / continue
│── Save and quit option

# ---- AFTER THE LOOP ----
Print trip summary
"Thanks for playing!"

checkpoints.title: Load City Distances

content.body: A function that reads city_distances.txt and creates a nested dictionary where you can look up distances like distances['San Antonio']['Houston'] → 200.

In Modules 1–3, you had 20+ constants like SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200. Adding a new city meant adding 5+ new constants AND updating multiple if-elif chains. With a nested dictionary loaded from a file, adding a city is just adding lines to the data file. Your code doesn't change at all.

references.description: You used .split('/') to break a date string into parts. Now use .split(',') to break each CSV line into city1, city2, and distance.
You created a dictionary with key-value pairs. Now you're building a nested dictionary where each city maps to another dictionary of destinations.

items: Create a new file called lab-4-1.py with your standard docstring.
Add imports: import random and import function_library.
Define load_city_distances(filename) that takes a filename parameter.
Create an empty dictionary inside the function: distances = {}.
Open the file and loop through each line.
For each line: use line.strip().split(',') to get a list of parts.
Extract: city1 = parts[0], city2 = parts[1], distance = int(parts[2]).
Check if city1 is already a key; if not, create an empty dictionary for it: if city1 not in distances: distances[city1] = {}.
Add the destination: distances[city1][city2] = distance.
Close the file and return the distances dictionary.

content.quote: When testing file-loaded data, I always check three things: Does the file load correctly? Is the data structured right? What happens if the file is malformed? Print your dictionary right after loading it — if the keys and values look right, your parsing worked. If not, you found the bug early.

rows: Lines have \n at the end. Use line.strip() first.
KeyError when adding to nested dict
Check if city1 exists first: if city1 not in distances:
Forgetting to convert distance to int

content.body: Step 1: Add this test harness at the bottom of your file:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 2
distances = load_city_distances('city_distances.txt')
print('Loaded cities:', list(distances.keys()))
print('San Antonio to Houston:', distances['San Antonio']['Houston'])
print('Houston destinations:', distances['Houston'])

content.body: Step 2: Run and verify this output:
Step 3: Verify the distance is an int (200) not a string ('200').
Step 4: DELETE the DEBUG lines before moving to Checkpoint 2.

• Docstring with your name, ID, section, and description
  • load_city_distances() function that returns a nested dictionary
  • Uses .strip() and .split(',') to parse each line
  • Can access any distance with distances[city1][city2]

checkpoints.subtitle: Parse the city_distances.txt file and build a nested dictionary structure.

checkpoints.title: Load City Info

content.body: A function that reads city_info.txt and creates a dictionary where each city maps to another dictionary containing its type and tagline. Like this: city_info['Houston']['tagline'] → 'Space City'.

references.description: You built a dictionary where card names map to values. Now build a dictionary where city names map to dictionaries of attributes.

items: Define load_city_info(filename) that takes a filename parameter.
Create an empty dictionary: info = {}.
Open the file and loop through each line.
For each line: use line.strip().split(',') to get a list of parts.
Extract: city = parts[0], city_type = parts[1], tagline = parts[2].
Create the nested entry: info[city] = {'type': city_type, 'tagline': tagline}.
Close the file and return the info dictionary.

content.body: Step 1: Add this test harness:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 3
city_info = load_city_info('city_info.txt')
print('Houston type:', city_info['Houston']['type'])
print('Houston tagline:', city_info['Houston']['tagline'])
print('Corpus Christi type:', city_info['Corpus Christi']['type'])

content.body: Step 2: Run and verify this output:

content.output:
Houston type: GATEWAY
Houston tagline: Space City
Corpus Christi type: WIN

content.body: Step 3: DELETE the DEBUG lines before moving to Checkpoint 3.

rows: Function must return info at the end

content.body:
• load_city_info() function that returns a dictionary of dictionaries
  • Each city maps to {'type': ..., 'tagline': ...}
  • Can access city attributes with city_info[city]['type']

checkpoints.subtitle: Parse the city_info.txt file and build a dictionary with nested city data.

checkpoints.title: Data-Driven Display

content.body: Functions that display available destinations from a city and help with navigation — all driven by your dictionaries, not hardcoded values.

items: Create display_destinations(city, distances) that takes the current city name and the distances dictionary.
Loop through the destinations using a counter variable to number each option (1, 2, 3…).
Print each option: number, destination name, and distance.
Create get_destination_by_number(city, distances, choice).
Convert dict keys to a list: destinations = list(distances[city].keys()).
Return the destination at index choice - 1 (user picks 1, but list index is 0).
Create is_win_city(city, city_info) that checks if city_info[city]['type'] equals 'WIN'.
Create is_dead_end(city, city_info) that checks if the type is 'DEAD_END' or 'WRONG_WAY'.

content.body: Step 1: Add this test harness:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 4
distances = load_city_distances('city_distances.txt')
city_info = load_city_info('city_info.txt')

display_destinations('San Antonio', distances)
dest = get_destination_by_number('San Antonio', distances, 2)
print('Choice 2 is:', dest)

print('Corpus Christi is win?', is_win_city('Corpus Christi', city_info))
print('Laredo is dead end?', is_dead_end('Laredo', city_info))

content.body: Step 2: Run and verify this output:

content.output:
From San Antonio, you can travel to:

  1. Corpus Christi - 150 miles
  2. Houston - 200 miles
  3. Austin - 80 miles
  4. Laredo - 160 miles
  5. El Paso - 550 miles

Choice 2 is: Houston
Corpus Christi is win? True
Laredo is dead end? True

content.body: Step 3: DELETE the DEBUG lines before moving to Checkpoint 4.

rows: User enters 1 for first item, but list(dict.keys())[0] is index 0. Use choice - 1.

content.body:
• display_destinations() shows a numbered menu from the dictionary
  • get_destination_by_number() converts user choice to destination name
  • is_win_city() returns True for WIN cities
  • is_dead_end() returns True for DEAD_END or WRONG_WAY cities

checkpoints.subtitle: Create helper functions that use dictionaries to display destinations and look up information.

checkpoints.title: Complete Game Loop

content.body: A complete game that loads data, gets the player's name, displays current city info, lets the player choose destinations, uses a coin flip gate, and tracks the route until they reach the beach or a dead end.

items: Create a main() function with a welcome banner.
Load both data files using your two loading functions.
Get the player's name: use input().strip() to clean whitespace.
Initialize game state: current_city = 'San Antonio', total_miles = 0, visited = [current_city].
Start the game loop: while playing:.
Show the current city name and its tagline from city_info.
Check for a win (is_win_city) or dead end (is_dead_end).
Display destinations and get the player's choice using function_library.get_valid_int().
Use the coin flip gate: HEADS = go to chosen city, TAILS = random redirect.
Update state: current_city, total_miles, and the visited list.
After the loop ends, print the final trip report.

content.title: Input Validation Is Data-Driven Too
content.body: Use function_library.get_valid_int(prompt, min, max) for the destination choice. The upper bound is len(distances[current_city]) — no hardcoding required.
Step 1: Add the main guard at the bottom of your file:
Step 2: Play through the game and verify:

• Data loads correctly (shows number of cities)
  • Current city shows tagline from the data file
  • Destination menu shows all options from the dictionary
  • Coin flip works (HEADS = choice, TAILS = random)
  • Reaching Corpus Christi or Galveston shows a win
  • Final report shows route and total miles

rows: Data files not in the same folder as the script
Python looks for files relative to where the script runs. Keep all .txt files with lab-4-1.py.
Not calling loading functions before using data
Call load_city_distances() and load_city_info() at the start of main().
Without this guard, main() runs when the module is imported, which breaks CodeGrade.

content.body:
• Complete main() function with game loop
  • All data comes from files (no hardcoded constants for cities)
  • Clean input with .strip()
  • Uses function_library for coin flip and input validation
  • Complete trip report at the end

checkpoints.subtitle: Wire everything together into a complete road trip game with the main() function.

beforeYouSubmit: Docstring includes your name, abc123, section, and description
Uses .strip() and .split(',') to parse file data
Uses dictionaries (not hardcoded constants) to store city data
All DEBUG/test code has been removed
No syntax errors when you run the program
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapters 2–9 only. String methods like .strip() and .split() are the Chapter 8 focus this week. .upper() and .lower() are saved for Module 4.2. Sets are saved for Module 4.3. CodeGrade will flag code using techniques from later chapters.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2–9 (Chapters 2–7 fully permitted from previous modules; Chapter 8 string methods .strip() and .split() as introduced this week; Chapter 9 dictionaries and nested dictionaries)
specific: File I/O: open(filename), reading lines in a loop
Dictionaries: creation, key access, nested dicts
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: Classes (Chapter 10), inheritance (Chapter 11), sets (Module 4.3), .upper() and .lower() (Module 4.2), list comprehensions

warnings: Do not use .upper() or .lower() yet — those are the Module 4.2 reveal. CodeGrade's scope check will flag premature use.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep lab-4-1.py — Module 4.2 adds string method input handling directly on top of your file-loading foundation. You will copy this file and build on it.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Program 8-10 (split_date.py) — the pattern for splitting CSV data is exactly what you need here
Re-read Program 9-1 (card_dealer.py) — creating and accessing dictionaries
Test each loading function independently — get load_city_distances() working before you start load_city_info()
Use DEBUG prints — print what you're parsing from each line to verify it's correct
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which tests pass or fail
